title: Over- and under-estimation of Riemann sums
description: Analyze Riemann sums of various functions to determine if they are an over-estimation or an under-estimation of the actual area.
topic_path: Over- and under-estimation of Riemann sums

assessment_items.question_content:
The continuous function $f$ is graphed.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 6 to 6. The graph is a smooth curve. The curve starts in quadrant 2, moves upward concave down, and ends in quadrant 1. The region between the curve and the x-axis, between x = negative 6 and x = 6, is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8773318fd99a79b2dd6ee50921d80600d4254022)

We're interested in the area under the curve between $x=-6$ and $x=6$, and we're considering using Riemann sums  to approximate it.

**Order the areas from least (on top) to greatest (on bottom).**

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
The function is increasing, so the left Riemann sum will underestimate the actual area:

![The graph of function f has the shaded area divided into 6 rectangles of equal width. Each rectangle touches the curve at the top left corner and is below the curve.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c06932dd37e3431c5a60e204723de5c271814444)

And the right Riemann sum will overestimate:

![The graph of function f has the shaded area divided into 6 rectangles of equal width. Each rectangle touches the curve at the top right corner and rises above the curve.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a316a16f2eaf689e5dd05ae5cdcbd6594f0b86c8)

Here are the areas ordered from least to greatest:

* Left Riemann sum
* Actual area under the curve
* Right Riemann sum

assessment_items.question_content:
This table gives select values of the continuous and decreasing function $f$.

$x$ | $2$ | $5$ | $8$ | $11$ | $14$
:- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$f(x)$ | $23$ | $16$ | $14$ | $7$ | $2$

We're interested in the area under the curve between $x=2$ and $x=14$, and we're considering using left and right Riemann sums, each with four equal subdivisions, to approximate it.

**Order the areas from least (on top) to greatest (on bottom).**

[[☃ sorter 1]]
hints:
Whether a Riemann sum approximation is an underestimation or an overestimation of the actual area depends on the direction of the function:

Direction | Left Riemann sum | Right Riemann sum
:- | :-: | :-:
Increasing | Underestimation | Overestimation
Decreasing | Overestimation | Underestimation

**Pro tip:** Don't memorize this table. Instead, try to visualize a graph of a Riemann sum and see why, for example, the area under the curve of a decreasing function would be overestimated by a left Riemann sum.

Since $f$ is decreasing, here are the areas ordered from least to greatest:

* Right Riemann sum
* Actual area under the curve
* Left Riemann sum
